draft.title: a CRM for freelancers
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a CRM for freelancers

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: A CRM for freelancers works better when the voice feels approachable and human, not corporate or stiff—using conversational language, warm copy, and a personality that feels like a helpful colleague rather than enterprise software.
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality: Friendly vs Professional
facade.label: Friendly and casual
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into conversational microcopy, warm error messages, personality in notifications, casual tone in help docs, and design language that feels like talking to a friend.
facade.rejectImplies: Shift toward a more formal, professional, business-grade tone—polished language, corporate vocabulary, neutral and efficient copy that prioritizes clarity over charm.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer soft, approachable interfaces that feel like a trusted partner rather than cold enterprise software
facade.label: Warm glow
facade.acceptImplies: Design should use warm color palettes, rounded edges, and personal touches to build emotional trust and reduce overwhelm
facade.rejectImplies: The CRM should feel more professional/clinical with cooler tones and structured layouts that signal efficiency over comfort

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer high-contrast layouts that separate navigation (dark, calm) from active workspace (bright, focus)
facade.label: Dark sidebar, light content
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward layouts with deliberate dark-light contrast zones — likely values clear visual hierarchy and reduced cognitive load in dense work UIs
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers flat contrast or monochromatic schemes — may value minimal visual drama, or wants uniformity across interface regions

evidence.hypothesis: A CRM for freelancers works better when the voice feels approachable and human, not corporate or stiff—using conversational language, warm copy, and a personality that feels like a helpful colleague rather than enterprise software.
evidence.implication: Lean into conversational microcopy, warm error messages, personality in notifications, casual tone in help docs, and design language that feels like talking to a friend.

facade.hypothesis: Freelance CRM users need persistent access to core sections (clients, projects, invoices) without sacrificing mobile responsiveness or content area.
facade.axisTargeted: Information architecture & layout pattern (sidebar vs top nav vs tabs)
facade.label: Sidebar navigation
facade.acceptImplies: User values vertical space efficiency, persistent context, and desktop-first workflow. Explore: collapsible sidebar, rail nav, dashboard prominence.
facade.rejectImplies: User prioritizes mobile-first design, horizontal scanability, or tabbed workflows. Explore: top navigation, card-based dashboards, bottom sheet patterns.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer visual scanning of client summaries (photo, name, status) over dense tabular rows for quick relationship overview
facade.label: Client list as cards
facade.acceptImplies: Design client views with card-based layouts, showing at-a-glance status and contact info; prioritize visual hierarchy and scannable chunks over spreadsheet density
facade.rejectImplies: Freelancers need comprehensive tabular data displays with sortable columns, filters, and bulk actions; card layouts waste screen real estate for information-heavy workflows

facade.hypothesis: Freelance CRM users expect client panels and modals to appear instantly with zero animation, prioritizing speed and directness over transition polish.
facade.label: Instant snap open
facade.acceptImplies: Interactions should favor snappy, zero-delay responses. Modals pop. Panels slide instantly. No easing or fade-in sequences. Speed signals control and confidence.
facade.rejectImplies: Smooth transitions and animated reveals are valued. Easing curves and duration matter. Users prefer fluid motion that telegraphs changes rather than jarring instant swaps.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a 'blank canvas' CRM or one with visual warmth and personality
Whether user wants tools clustered in one sidebar/dashboard vs. spread across tabs and modals—reveals navigation preference for a freelancer juggling multiple clients
Whether user responds to professional/formal tone vs. casual/encouraging tone—reveals if they want a business tool or a supportive coach
Whether user wants a calm, distraction-free workspace vs. energetic, motivational environment—reveals stress tolerance and work style for solo freelancers
Whether user prefers numbers/metrics front-and-center vs. narrative summaries and stories—reveals how they track success and make decisions
Whether user wants instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals patience level and preference for control vs. automation

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a 'blank canvas' CRM or one with visual warmth and personality
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants tools clustered in one sidebar/dashboard vs. spread across tabs and modals—reveals navigation preference for a freelancer juggling multiple clients
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to professional/formal tone vs. casual/encouraging tone—reveals if they want a business tool or a supportive coach
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants a calm, distraction-free workspace vs. energetic, motivational environment—reveals stress tolerance and work style for solo freelancers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers numbers/metrics front-and-center vs. narrative summaries and stories—reveals how they track success and make decisions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals patience level and preference for control vs. automation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to interface elements that feel approachable and slightly whimsical—icons and micro-interactions that hint at personality without sacrificing clarity, reducing software fatigue in repetitive CRM tasks.
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — playful vs serious
facade.label: Playful icons
facade.acceptImplies: Double down on custom icons, animated micro-interactions, emoji-adjacent visual language, and interactions that feel delightful rather than mechanical. Personality through detail.
facade.rejectImplies: Stick to neutral, minimal iconography. Personality lives in copy and tone, not visual flourishes. Icons should be invisible (clarity first, no character).
